title: NOC 94110 – Chemical plant machine operators | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s chemical manufacturing sector is a high-tech pillar of the national economy, supporting everything from pharmaceutical production to the development of sustainable biofuels. Chemical Plant Machine Operators (NOC 94110) are the precision-focused professionals who monitor and control the equipment that processes raw materials into essential chemical products. With Canada's 2026 focus on "Green Chemistry" and localized supply chains, international operators with experience in automated batch processing and safety-critical environments are seeing a surge in high-wage job offers.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Occupations usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Processing and manufacturing machine operatorsSub-Major Group941 – Machine operators in mineral and metal processing

duties: Machine Operation: Monitoring and operating specialized machinery to spray, coat, granulate, or pelletize chemical products.
Batch Preparation: Measuring and loading raw materials into mixers or reactors according to precise chemical formulas.
Process Control: Adjusting controls to regulate temperature, pressure, and flow rates of chemicals through the production line.
Quality Assurance: Taking samples of products for laboratory analysis and performing routine tests (pH, viscosity, density).
2026 Update: Utilizing digital tablets to monitor real-time SCADA alerts and logging environmental compliance data for "Net-Zero" reporting.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in the same company is often preferred.
Training: Several months of on-the-job training are typically provided by the employer.
Certifications: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System). A Power Engineering Certificate (4th or 5th class) is a massive asset for career progression.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market nationally, but "Shortages" in specialized chemical manufacturing hubs. As Canada’s pharmaceutical and bio-product exports increase, over 6,000 new openings are projected due to industry growth and a wave of retirements in the Sarnia and Montreal regions.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for chemical machine operators is rated as Good to Very Good, particularly in industrial corridors:
Ontario (Sarnia & GTA): High demand. Sarnia’s "Bio-Industrial Innovation Centre" and the GTA's pharmaceutical cluster are actively recruiting international talent to address labor shortages.
Alberta (Edmonton/Strathcona County): Very Good prospects. Driven by massive investments in the "Alberta Carbon Trunk Line" and downstream chemical processing.
Quebec: Strong demand. Focused in the Greater Montreal area, supporting the life sciences and specialized chemical sectors.
Saskatchewan: Stable demand. Essential for the processing of industrial chemicals used in the province’s global-scale potash and uranium sectors.
Why Work in Canada as a Chemical Plant Operator?
Innovation-Driven Sector: Work in facilities transitioning to AI-monitored processing, carbon-capture technology, and sustainable "Green Chemistry" protocols.
Market-Leading Salaries: Chemical processing remains one of the highest-paying technical fields in Canada for TEER 4 roles.
Comprehensive Safety Culture: Canada’s 2026 industrial safety standards are among the highest globally, ensuring a secure and regulated work environment.
Defined PR Pathways: This NOC is frequently listed in PNP In-Demand categories and is a core qualifier for the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
pathways: Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): NOC 94110 is an eligible occupation for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s industrial sectors.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: A reliable pathway for operators currently working in Alberta’s thriving petrochemical and chemical hubs.
TR to PR Pathway (2026): Chemical plant operators are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them high-priority candidates for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical machine operators to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
